content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 30 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 228–229 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_030_page_001.jpg, DEU_030_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 162 — DEU_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg

---

**1** And it shall be, when all these things come upon you — the blessing and the curse that I have set before you — and you take [them] to heart [vahasheivota el-levavecha] among all the nations where the LORD your God has driven you,

**2** and you return to the LORD your God and listen to his voice — according to all that I am commanding you today — you and your sons — with all your heart and with all your being [nephesh],

**3** then the LORD your God will restore your fortunes [shuv et-shevutcha] and have compassion on you, and he will return and gather you from all the peoples where the LORD your God has scattered you.

**4** If your outcasts are at the ends of the heavens, from there the LORD your God will gather you, and from there he will take you.

**5** And the LORD your God will bring you into the land that your fathers possessed, and you shall possess it; and he will do you good and multiply you more than your fathers.

**6** And the LORD your God will circumcise your heart and the heart of your offspring, to love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your being [nephesh] — so that you may live.

**7** And the LORD your God will put all these curses upon your enemies and upon those who hate you and who persecuted you.

**8** And you shall return and listen to the voice of the LORD and do all his commandments that I am commanding you today.

**9** And the LORD your God will make you abound in all the work of your hand, in the fruit of your womb and in the fruit of your livestock and in the fruit of your ground — for good; for the LORD will again rejoice over you for good, as he rejoiced over your fathers —

**10** when you listen to the voice of the LORD your God, to keep his commandments and his statutes that are written in this scroll of the Torah — when you return to the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your being [nephesh].

**11** For this commandment that I am commanding you today is not too difficult for you [lo nifle'at hi mimcha], and it is not far off.

**12** It is not in the heavens, that you should say: Who will go up for us to the heavens and take it for us and cause us to hear it, that we may do it?

**13** And it is not beyond the sea, that you should say: Who will cross over for us to the other side of the sea and take it for us and cause us to hear it, that we may do it?

**14** For the word is very near to you — in your mouth and in your heart — to do it.

**15** See, I have set before you today life and good, and death and evil —

**16** in that I am commanding you today to love the LORD your God, to walk in his ways, and to keep his commandments and his statutes and his ordinances, that you may live and multiply, and the LORD your God will bless you in the land that you are entering to possess.

**17** But if your heart turns away and you do not listen, and you are drawn away and bow down to other gods and serve them —

**18** I declare to you today that you shall surely perish; you shall not prolong your days on the land that you are crossing the Yarden to enter and possess.

**19** I call to witness against you today the heavens and the earth: life and death I have set before you, the blessing and the curse — and you shall choose life [u-vacharta ba-chayyim], so that you may live, you and your offspring —

**20** to love the LORD your God, to listen to his voice, and to cling to him; for he is your life and the length of your days, for you to dwell upon the land that the LORD swore to your fathers — to Avraham, to Yitschak, and to Ya'akov — to give to them.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.1 — "Take to heart" (vahasheivota el-levavecha):** Literally "you shall return [it] to your heart" — the reflexive turning of the mind toward the situation. EMET renders this as "take [them] to heart," reflecting the Hebrew idiom of internalization.

**V.3 — "Restore your fortunes" (shuv et-shevutcha):** The phrase is a paronomasia — the verb shuv ("return/restore") and the noun shevut ("captivity/fortunes") share the same root. The meaning is debated: (a) "restore your captivity" — i.e., bring back those in exile; (b) "restore your fortune/condition." LXX: ιασεται κυριος τας αμαρτιας σου — "the LORD will heal your sins" — a striking interpretive divergence, shifting from "restore fortunes" to "forgive sins." This is a major LXX expansion of the sense.

**V.6 — "Circumcise your heart" (umol YHWH Eloheicha et-levavecha):** A key eschatological promise. Unlike Deut 10:16 where Yisra'el is commanded to circumcise their own hearts, here the LORD does it — indicating divine action in the restoration. LXX: περιτεμει κυριος ο θεος σου την καρδιαν σου — exact. Paul alludes to this in Rom 2:29 (circumcision of the heart by the Spirit).

**V.11 — "Not too difficult/wonderful" (lo nifle'at):** Nifle'at derives from pala' — "to be wonderful/extraordinary/beyond ordinary capacity." EMET renders "not too difficult for you" (the most common English gloss) but notes the literal sense is "not beyond-wonderful from you" — meaning not hidden in mystery. LXX: η εντολη αυτη... ουχ υπερογκος εστιν — "this commandment is not excessive/too great."

**V.12–14 — Torah not in heaven:** This passage became foundational in rabbinic thought (b. Bava Metsi'a 59b — "lo ba-shamayim hi" as the proof that the Torah has been given to human decision). EMET renders "in your mouth and in your heart" precisely — both oral and interior aspects of Torah. Paul quotes v.14 in Rom 10:8 applied to the "word of faith."

**V.15 — Life and good; death and evil:** The paired antitheses — chayyim ve-tov, mavet va-ra' — structure the whole choice of ch. 30. EMET preserves the exact word order: life precedes good (not "good and life"), death precedes evil. This is not the typical moral order but the existential one.

**V.19 — "U-vacharta ba-chayyim" — Choose life:** The climactic imperative of Deuteronomy. Ba-chayyim means literally "in the life" — choose into/within life. EMET renders: "you shall choose life." LXX: εκλεξαι την ζωην — "choose life" — confirms the imperative force. The verse functions as Deuteronomy's central hortatory summary.

**V.19 — Heaven and earth as witnesses:** The invocation of shamayim va-aretz as cosmic witnesses to the covenant echoes 4:26 and 31:28. LXX: μαρτυρομαι υμιν σημερον τον τε ουρανον και την γην — "I call as witnesses against you today both heaven and earth." The forensic language of covenant ratification.

**V.20 — "He is your life and the length of your days" (ki hu chayyeicha ve-orech yameicha):** The LORD himself is identified as the source and substance of life, not merely its giver. EMET renders this literally: "for he is your life." LXX: τουτο γαρ η ζωη σου — "for this is your life."

— EMET
